title: Chapter 6 Master Quiz
description: Test your mastery of functions, parameters, scope, and returns.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of writing a function?
options: To make the code run slower
To group code into reusable blocks so you don't repeat yourself
To create variables that can be seen everywhere
To print things to the console
questions.explanation: Functions act like little factories. We wrap code inside them so we can easily reuse it again and again.

questions.question: What happens immediately after you DEFINE a function: function sayHi() { console.log('Hi'); } ?
questions.explanation: Defining a function only builds the machine. It will sit completely dormant until you physically invoke (call) it later in the code.

questions.question: Which represents a valid function CALL (Execution)?
questions.explanation: To call a function, you must write its name followed immediately by parentheses ().

questions.question: When defining a function: function greet(name)—what do we call 'name'?
questions.explanation: When establishing the function, the placeholder variable in the () is called a Parameter. It acts as a catcher's mitt for data.

questions.question: When calling a function: greet("Alice")—what do we call "Alice"?
questions.explanation: When invoking the function, the actual concrete data you pass into it is called an Argument.

questions.question: If a function is expecting 3 parameters, but you only provide 2 arguments, what value is assigned to the 3rd parameter?
questions.explanation: JavaScript will not crash. It will simply create the 3rd parameter as 'undefined' and attempt to run the function normally.

questions.question: You can assign a 'default value' to a parameter so that it doesn't accidentally become 'undefined'.
questions.explanation: By putting an equals sign inside the parameters (e.g., function greet(name = 'Guest')), you prevent undefined errors.

questions.question: What keyword is required to pass data back OUT of a function when it finishes running?
questions.explanation: The 'return' keyword instantly stops the function and sends the adjacent value back to wherever the function was originally called.

questions.question: What happens if you place console.log("Done") on the line IMMEDIATELY AFTER a return statement?
options: It never prints because the function was instantly destroyed by 'return'
questions.explanation: The 'return' keyword acts identically to a 'break' statement. As soon as it runs, the function dies, ignoring any code underneath it.

questions.question: If a variable is created inside the main file (Global Scope), can a function created lower down in the file use it?
options: Yes, global variables can be seen by inner blocks
No, functions can only use their own local variables
Yes, but only if they use 'var'
No, trying to use it creates a circular error
questions.explanation: Scope works like a one-way mirror. Inner blocks (like functions) can freely look OUT at the global scope and access its variables.

questions.question: If a variable `let x = 5;` is created INSIDE a function (Local Scope), can the main file print it?,
options: Yes, the main file sees everything
No, the main file cannot look IN to a local block's variable
Yes, but only if the function is still running
questions.explanation: Local variables act like black boxes. They are hidden from the outside world and destroyed when the block finishes execution.

questions.question: The '=>' operator is known as the 'fat arrow' and is used to create modern ES6 Arrow Functions.
questions.explanation: Arrow functions are a shorter, sleeker syntax for writing functions introduced in JavaScript ES6.

questions.question: What is the equivalent arrow function for: function add(a, b) { return a + b; } ?
questions.explanation: const name = (params) => implicit_return_math; is the cleanest way to write a single-line arrow function.

questions.question: What requires you to explicitly type the word 'return' in an Arrow Function?
options: Nothing, you never have to type it again
Adding curly braces {} to create a multi-line block
questions.explanation: Implicit return ONLY works if you omit the curly braces. If you add {} to an arrow function, it behaves like a normal function and needs 'return'.

questions.question: Variables created outside of any curly braces {} live in the ______ Scope.
questions.explanation: These variables act like the sun, visible to every other function and block in the file.

questions.question: Variables created inside a function or an if-statement live in the _____ Scope.
questions.explanation: Also called Block Scope, these variables are completely trapped inside their `{}` brackets.

questions.question: Does `let answer = Math.random();` contain a function call?
options: No, it's just generating a random object.
No, it is assigning a string.
questions.explanation: The parentheses () at the end of Math.random() prove that we are executing a function.

questions.question: If you forget to provide a return statement in a function, it will throw a critical ReferenceError when called.
questions.explanation: It won't crash! It simply returns the `undefined` value natively.

questions.question: What is the primary visual difference between a traditional function definition and an arrow function?
options: Arrow functions do not use the 'function' keyword.
Arrow functions cannot have variables inside them.
Arrow functions must be written on 5 lines.
questions.explanation: Arrow functions drop the 'function' keyword entirely, replacing it with the sleek '=>' arrow.

questions.question: Is it possible to pass a function as an argument INTO another function?
options: Yes! This is highly common in JavaScript.
Only if it is an Arrow Function.
questions.explanation: Yes! We call these 'Callbacks', and they are a massive part of Advanced JavaScript.
